title: US Federal Sector-Specific AI Rules and Guidance
official_title: Sector-specific federal agency guidance and rules on artificial intelligence: CFPB, EEOC, FDA, OCC, HHS
overview: Beyond the FTC, multiple US federal agencies with sector-specific jurisdiction have issued binding rules, guidance, and enforcement statements on AI within their domains. These obligations apply in addition to horizontal frameworks like EO 14110 and the NIST AI RMF. Companies in regulated industries face layered obligations from their sector regulator on top of cross-sector AI rules. This baseline covers CFPB (financial services), EEOC (employment), FDA (healthcare/medical devices), OCC (banking/model risk), and HHS (healthcare generally).

sector_frameworks.sector: Consumer financial services — credit, lending, debt collection

key_rules.title: ECOA / Regulation B — AI Adverse Action Notices
key_rules.description: When AI or algorithmic systems deny credit, increase cost, or take other adverse actions against consumers, creditors must provide specific and accurate reasons — not generic statements. The CFPB has stated that 'model output' is not an acceptable reason. Each actual reason the model used must be disclosed in plain language.
obligations: Identify and disclose the specific factors the AI model used in making the adverse decision
Reasons must be accurate and reflect the actual model logic — not just the top output factors from a post-hoc explainability tool
Generic reasons such as 'credit score' or 'model output' are insufficient if a more specific reason is identifiable
Adverse action notices must be provided within 30 days of the decision
key_rules.enforcement_risk: High — CFPB actively supervises AI-based credit decisions

key_rules.title: FCRA — AI as Consumer Report
key_rules.description: AI systems that compile consumer information and provide assessments to creditors, employers, or others may constitute consumer reporting agencies (CRAs) subject to FCRA. This triggers accuracy, dispute, and adverse action requirements.
obligations: Determine whether the AI system meets the FCRA definition of a consumer report
If so: implement FCRA accuracy obligations, dispute processes, and consumer consent requirements
Sellers of AI scoring products used in credit/employment/insurance decisions must register as CRAs
Provide adverse action notices under both ECOA and FCRA when applicable

key_rules.title: UDAAP — AI Discriminatory Practices
key_rules.description: The CFPB has stated that discrimination in consumer financial products can constitute an Unfair, Deceptive, or Abusive Act or Practice (UDAAP) under the Consumer Financial Protection Act, regardless of whether it also violates ECOA. AI-driven disparate impact on protected classes can trigger UDAAP enforcement.
obligations: Test AI-based financial products for discriminatory impact on protected classes
Document testing methodology and results
Remediate products producing significant disparate impact

key_rules.title: EEOC Technical Assistance on AI in Employment
key_rules.description: The EEOC has issued technical assistance documents clarifying that Title VII of the Civil Rights Act, the ADA, the ADEA, and the EPA apply fully to AI-assisted employment decisions. Employers cannot rely on vendor representations that an AI tool is bias-free.
obligations: Employers are liable for discriminatory AI tools even when purchased from third-party vendors
Conduct adverse impact analysis before deploying AI selection tools
Use the 4/5ths rule (80% threshold) as a baseline for identifying potential disparate impact
Job-relatedness and business necessity must be demonstrated if the tool produces adverse impact
ADA requires reasonable accommodation in AI-based screening — e.g. alternative testing for candidates with disabilities

key_rules.title: ADA and AI — Disability Discrimination
key_rules.description: AI tools used in hiring may screen out candidates with disabilities through inaccessible interfaces, questions about medical history, or assessments that penalise disability-related traits. The EEOC has specifically warned about video interview AI, personality assessments, and cognitive ability tests.
obligations: Provide notice that accommodation is available for AI-based assessments
Offer alternative assessment methods for candidates who cannot complete the AI-based process
Do not use AI tools that measure traits correlated with protected disabilities as proxies for job performance

sector_frameworks.sector: Healthcare — medical devices, diagnostics, clinical decision support

key_rules.title: AI/ML-Based Software as a Medical Device (SaMD)
key_rules.description: FDA regulates AI systems that are intended to diagnose, treat, prevent, or monitor medical conditions as Software as a Medical Device (SaMD). Risk-based classification determines the approval pathway: Class I (510(k) exempt), Class II (510(k) clearance), or Class III (PMA approval).
obligations: Determine whether the AI system meets the definition of a medical device or SaMD
If yes: register with FDA and select appropriate regulatory pathway based on risk classification
AI/ML-based SaMD that continuously learns and adapts must comply with the FDA's predetermined change control plan (PCCP) framework
Post-market surveillance required — report adverse events and device malfunctions
Labeling must describe the AI/ML model's intended use, performance, and limitations

key_rules.title: FDA AI/ML-Based SaMD Action Plan
key_rules.description: FDA's 2021 action plan and subsequent guidance documents establish the framework for overseeing AI systems that adapt over time through continued learning.
obligations: Submit a Predetermined Change Control Plan (PCCP) for adaptive AI that specifies anticipated modifications
Algorithm change protocols must be approved before implementing model updates that could affect device performance
Real-world performance monitoring required for deployed AI medical devices
Report performance degradation events within 30 days

sector_frameworks.agency: Office of the Comptroller of the Currency (OCC) / Federal Reserve / FDIC

key_rules.title: SR 11-7 — Model Risk Management
key_rules.description: The Federal Reserve's SR 11-7 guidance (adopted by OCC and FDIC) establishes model risk management (MRM) requirements for bank models, including AI models used in credit decisions, fraud detection, AML, and operations. AI systems are models subject to MRM governance.
obligations: Maintain a model inventory including all AI systems used in material business decisions
Conduct independent model validation before deployment and periodically thereafter
Document model development, including training data, assumptions, limitations, and validation results
Establish model governance framework with board-level oversight
Assess model risk regularly — at least annually for high-risk models
Maintain model risk appetite and report material model risk to senior management

sector_frameworks.agency: Department of Health and Human Services (HHS)

key_rules.title: Section 1557 — Non-Discrimination in Health Programs Using AI
key_rules.description: HHS regulations under Section 1557 of the ACA require health programs receiving federal funding (virtually all US hospitals and health insurers) to not discriminate on the basis of race, color, national origin, sex, age, or disability. HHS has clarified that algorithmic tools that produce discriminatory clinical recommendations violate Section 1557.
obligations: Assess AI-based clinical decision support tools for discriminatory outputs before deployment
Establish oversight mechanisms for AI tools used in patient care decisions
Provide reasonable modifications to standard AI-based procedures for patients with disabilities
Document algorithmic bias assessments and corrective actions

cross_references.relevance: CFPB UDAAP enforcement overlaps with FTC deception and unfairness authority; both agencies can pursue AI discrimination in consumer products.
US companies with EU operations face dual obligations: sector-specific US rules plus EU AI Act high-risk classifications for healthcare, credit, and employment AI.
Federal agency AI use obligations in EO 14110 reinforce sector-specific regulator requirements; NIST AI RMF aligns with SR 11-7 model risk management approach.
